result:
I want to tell you about the tiny, humming universe that lives inside the plastic housing of my office surge-protector. Most people see it as a $14 lump of rubberized beige whose sole job is to accept six plugs and forgive thunderstorms. I see a skyline: five green LEDs arranged like a constellation, each one a planet that flickers its own dialect of quiet optimism. When I lie on the carpet at 2:18 a.m.—the hour when every unsent email grows fangs—those five lights are the only witnesses to the fact that I am still trying. The left-most one is always the bravest; it stays lit even when the overhead fluorescents stutter during storms, a stoic little Cerberus keeping the abyss from chewing through my laptop charger. I have, on three separate occasions, said “thank you” out loud to that diode. I am not embarrassed by this.

We live in a world so saturated with electricity that we forget it is weather. Electrons are just tiny storms, pressure fronts of charge migrating from clouds of lithium across copper valleys to oceans of ground. Yet every night I flip the bedroom switch and expect photons to appear the way ancient farmers expected dawn: reliable enough to bet your life on, invisible enough to ignore. I bet my life on it every time I plug in the CPAP mask that keeps my heart from forgetting its rhythm while I sleep. The machine exhales for me at 11 breaths per minute, a mechanical monk reciting the simplest of mantras: stay, stay, stay. I have never met the people in Shenzhen who laminated the circuit boards inside that machine, but I have dreamed of them—faceless guardians threading copper like red silk through digital rosaries. Once, after a hurricane knocked the grid sideways for six humid days, I lost three pounds because my lungs kept waking me up, startled by the novelty of having to remember breathing. On the fourth morning I drove to the library to charge the battery, and the librarian let me sit in the staff break room, where the vending machine hummed the same B-flat my mother used to hum while folding laundry. I cried into a Styrofoam cup of free coffee, tasting cathode and anode and the strange mercy of strangers.

Memory is also a kind of voltage. It accumulates in the body’s capacitors until some threshold is crossed, then arcs across decades. When I was eight I built a lemon battery for the fourth-grade science fair—copper penny, zinc-galvanized nail, sour fruit. I wired four of them in series to power a red LED the size of a breadcrumb. The bulb glowed for exactly eleven minutes before the classroom’s fluorescent lights absorbed its will to shine. I remember the smell of lemon rind under my fingernails more vividly than the face of the girl who won first prize for her erupting papier-mâché volcano. That metallic citrus ghost follows me through grocery produce aisles, a private aurora. Sometimes I stand by the pyramid of imported lemons and wonder how many millivolts of childhood are still trapped in their pith, how many nights I could illuminate if I juiced the entire display into a Mason jar battery farm.

We measure childhood in strange units: the half-life of glitter, the torque required to snap a crayon, the decibel of sidewalk chalk on asphalt. Adulthood arrives calibrated in kilowatt-hours, in deductible percentages, in the exact number of missed calls that flip a parent from “living” to “deceased” on your phone’s contact list. My father’s entry still says “Dad Cell,” even though the line now belongs to a stranger who answers in Mandarin and hangs up, startled by English grief. I have pressed that digital button a hundred times by accident while scrolling, the muscle memory of longing disguised as clumsy thumbs. The phone bill still lists the last 90 days of calls; I download the PDF each month and save it to a folder labeled “Archaeology,” as if some future civilization will carbon-date my inability to delete him.

There is a small rebellion in refusing to upgrade. My laptop is seven years old, its aluminum case pitted like the surface of the moon. The fan sounds like a distant motorcycle rally, but it still compiles code, still renders the 3-D model of the coffee mug I designed in Blender because I needed proof I could finish something. The mug is intentionally lopsided, a ceramic shrug that says perfection is a flavor I no longer crave. I printed it at the library makerspace—layer by layer of corn-based PLA, the hot nozzle describing each micron like a prayer wheel. The librarian, a woman named Dori, hovered nervously because I muttered “sorry” every time the printer squeaked. She told me her husband died of pancreatic cancer the previous winter and now she volunteers every Saturday because the smell of melting plastic reminds her of the model trains they never got around to building in the basement. We stood in companionable silence watching the nozzle trace a spiral that would become a handle, both of us manufacturing small, useful sorrow.

I read once that the human heart’s electrical field can be detected three feet away, a cloud of potential that strangers walk through without noticing. I keep thinking about the cumulative charge of every commuter on the subway at 7:43 a.m., all those overlapping storms contained inside aluminum shells hurtling downtown. If you could see it, the car would glow like a paper lantern, each passenger a filament pulsing jealousy, arousal, dread, hope. I want to believe that when we pack shoulder-to-shoulder we trade a few microvolts of grief, that your calmer waveform can cancel my arrhythmia, a human phase-shift. Instead we stare at notification rectangles, practicing the sacred art of not touching. Some mornings I carry a roll of peppermint Life Savers specifically to offer one to whoever is crying silently beside me; sugar and menthol are conductors, a two-factor authentication for kindness. Last Tuesday a woman in a teal hijab accepted, lips trembling, then reached into her purse and handed me a tiny plastic dinosaur wearing a knitted scarf. “His name is Thursday,” she said, already exiting at 34th Street. He stands guard now above my surge-protector metropolis, a saurian patron saint of strangers sharing voltage.

I want to write the way electricity would if it had thumbs: urgent, luminescent, incapable of lasting more than one heartbeat beyond its source. Instead I draft sentences the way squirrels bury nuts—obsessively, forgetfully, hoping some will sprout when I’m not looking. The problem is that words don’t obey Ohm’s law; they resist, they heat, they leak into unintended ground. I once spent an entire afternoon trying to describe the color of the sky after a midsummer storm and ended up with a paragraph about my grandmother’s Jell-O salad, how the blueberries hovered suspended like tiny storm clouds in lime-green sky. Readers assumed it was a metaphor for grief; I was just hungry and inaccurate. Still, I keep chasing the moment when syntax arcs across the insurmountable gap between two minds, that tiny audible click when someone else’s neural breaker flips from open to closed. It feels like the instant a fluorescent bulb decides to catch, the flicker just before the steady hum.

At 1:00 a.m. the apartment building enters its own circadian rhythm: compressor valves sigh, the refrigerator performs a minor-key solo, someone upstairs drags what might be a body across hardwood. My surge-pro protector keeps humming its five-note reassurance. I wonder how many gigabytes of unfinished novels, unwatched documentaries, unsent apologies are stored on hard drives spinning inches above this carpet, each one a tiny gyroscope of postponed life. The LEDs blink in quiet Morse: maybe tomorrow, maybe tomorrow. Outside, the city grid balances on a knife-edge of supply and demand; engineers in a control room two states away nudge generators like dealers coaxing roulette wheels. If they fail, the stars will still burn, but we will forget how to see them behind the automated billboards. I keep a hand-crank flashlight in the drawer beside the bed, a small plastic pope of preparedness. Every few months I test it, and the whirr of the dynamo feels like forgiveness: thirty seconds of muscle converting oatmeal and anxiety into light. I imagine the flashlight’s beam traveling outward at 299,792,458 meters per second, carrying a dossier of my face across light-years of dust. In 2.5 million years it will reach the Andromeda galaxy; by then humans may be extinct, but some alien insomniac might detect a faint stroboscopic hello and wonder who was terrified of the dark.

Which brings me back to breathing. The CPAP machine’s algorithm has learned my pattern; it auto-adjusts the airway pressure when it senses I’m sinking into REM. We are, together, a cyborg in love with its own circuitry, a closed-loop of lung and silicon. Sometimes I wake to find the mask hissing softly like a cat that has decided not to bite. In that twilight I remember that electricity was never the opposite of flesh, merely its distant cousin visiting from a country where time runs faster. Electrons drift at about one millimeter per second, yet the signal races close to light-speed because each one nudges the next like parishioners passing news down a pew. I picture the congregation inside my thorax: trillions of ionized faithful, passing the word breathe, breathe, breathe until the message reaches the diaphragm’s dome. Faith is just another word for circuitry we haven’t mapped yet.

The sun is beginning to smear the horizon tangerine; birds are rebooting their song files. Soon the LEDs on the surge-protector will pale, their dominion surrendered to daylight and the microwave clock’s smug green. I will unplug the laptop, close the lid on another night that refused to be tamed into coherence. Somewhere in the building a garage door rolls open like a metal tide. Someone’s coffee maker begins its first-cycle gurgle, the secular equivalent of a monastery bell. I want to tell them about Thursday the dinosaur, about lemon ghosts and voltage-based mercy, but language is a leaky bucket and the elevator is not a confessional. So I save the draft, press save, listen for the faint click of electrons settling like snow into capacitors. The five green lights keep vigil, miniature lighthouses steering lost ships of thought toward the safe harbor of another day.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
